title: What Demarcates Necropolitics from Biopolitics? A Foucauldian Critique of Mbembe
author: D'Cruz

opening_move.description: Mbembe's 'necropolitics' presents itself as a corrective to Foucault's biopolitics, but it is not clear that it succeeds: neither in demonstrating Foucault's deficiencies nor in demarcating necropolitics as a distinct modality of power. The paper diagnoses a fundamental misreading of Foucault.
opening_move.stakes_claimed: If necropolitics cannot be demarcated from biopolitics, the question of how to operationalize these frameworks to analyse racialized violence remains unresolved — and the most powerful conceptual tools remain Foucault's own.

rivals_engaged: secondary literature attempting to demarcate the two
theoretical_positioning.description: The paper defends Foucault against Mbembe's critique, arguing that Foucault's concepts of State racism and thanatopolitics already capture what necropolitics claims to add, but salvages Mbembe's descriptive concepts (death-worlds, living dead) as supplements within the Foucauldian framework.

epistemic_mode.interpretive_methods: Immanent philosophical critique: close reading of Foucault's lectures and Mbembe's texts to identify misreadings and assess whether necropolitics constitutes a distinct modality of power.
epistemic_mode.notes: Purely conceptual argument — no empirical material. The paper is a careful textual-philosophical analysis of two theoretical frameworks.

normative_stake.domain: political theory, theory of racialized violence

confidence_notes: Textbook immanent critique: shows that Mbembe's critique of Foucault rests on a misreading, that necropower is not a distinct modality, but preserves Mbembe's descriptive contributions. Purely conceptual — no case, no empirical material. The conclusion is confident and clear-cut ('Mbembe is wrong'), making the closure_type strong_normative_conclusion.
